coredata.dc:title: Self-assembled dual-emissive nanoprobe with metal−organic frameworks as scaffolds for enhanced ascorbic acid and ascorbate oxidase sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Junyang
Jiang, Shan
Wang, Mengke
Xie, Xiaolei
Su, Xingguang
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe was successfully fabricated through incapsulating quantum dots (QDs) and carbon dots (CDs) into the cavities of zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 by self-assembly, which generated QDs/CDs@MOFs. The QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe combined the properties of metal − organic frameworks (MOFs) and ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe, thus it possessed following advantages including strong adsorption and enrichment ability for the analytes, and the ability of eliminating interferences of environmental and instrumental factors. The fluorescence of QDs loaded in the cages of ZIF-8 was initially quenched by mercury ions. However, mercury ions could be reduced to mercury atom by ascorbic acid (AA), leading to the restoration of QDs fluorescence. Moreover, under the catalysis of ascorbate oxidase (AAO), AA was oxidized to dehydroascorbic acid that couldn’t reduce mercury ions, resulting in the fluorescence of QDs quenched again. Simultaneously, the fluorescence of CDs loaded in the ZIF-8 was not affected under above procedures. Hence, with QDs serving as reporter signal and CDs acting as stable reference signal, a ratio fluorescence platform was constructed for sensitive determination of AA and AAO with the linear calibration in the range of 0.01−0.2 μM and 0.05–4 U·L−1 and the detection limit of 4 nM and 0.02 U·L−1, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe
Mercury ions
Ascorbic acid
Ascorbate oxidase
Ratiometric

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detections of AA and AAO activity
Determination of AAO activity in human serum
Selectivity, anti-interference and real sample analysis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Development project of Jilin province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21775052 and 21575048 ) and the Science and Technology Development project of Jilin province, China (No. 20180414013GH ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Photos of the synthesized ZIF-8 (upper) and QDs/CDs@MOFs (down) under daylight (left) and 365 nm ultraviolet lamp. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of QDs, CDs, ZIF-8, the mixture of QDs and CDs (QDs/CDs), and QDs/CDs@MOFs. (The concentrations of QDs, CDs, ZIF-8, and QDs/CDs@MOFs were 0.001, 0.0002, 0.05 and 0.05 mg/mL, respectively) (C) TEM image of QDs/CDs@MOFs and the insert display the structure model. (D) HR-TEM image of QDs/CDs@MOFs.
(A) PXRD patterns of the prepared ZIF-8 and QDs/CDs@MOFs. (B) FT-IR spectra of QDs, CDs, pure ZIF-8, and QDs/CDs@MOFs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of QDs/CDs@MOFs or QDs/CDs in the absence and presence of 0.2 μM Hg2+. (B) Fluorescence spectra of QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe treated with Hg2+, Hg2+/AA, and Hg2+/AA/AAO (concentration: 0.2 μM Hg2+, 0.2 μM AA, and 4 U·L−1 AAO). (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of CdTe QDs, CdTe QDs+Hg2+, and CdTe QDs+Hg2++AA system. (D) Fluorescence lifetimes of QDs/CDs@MOFs without or with Hg2+or Hg2+/AA addition.

(A)Fluorescence spectra of QDs/CDs@MOFs/Hg2+ system treated with various amounts of AA from 0 to 0.30 μM (0, 0.01, 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.1, 0.15, 0.2, 0.3 μM). (B) The linear plot of fluorescence ratio (F615/F455) intensity versus the concentrations of AA. (C) Fluorescence spectra of QDs/CDs@MOFs/Hg2+/AA system treated with different levels of AAO from 0 to 6 U·L−1 (0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 6 U·L−1). (D) The linearity of fluorescent ratio (F615/F455) intensity versus AAO concentration.

(A) Selectivity of the QDs/CDs@MOFs/Hg2+/AA system for AAO analysis. (B) Anti-interference assay of the QDs/CDs@MOFs/Hg2+/AA system toward different coexisting interfering substances. Concentration: 4 U·L−1 AAO, 20 U·L−1 other bio-enzymes, 50 μM biomolecule, and 100 μM common ions.
Schematic representation of the synthesis of QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe and its application for sensing.
The determination results of AAO activity in human serum.

head.ce:title: Self-assembled dual-emissive nanoprobe with metal−organic frameworks as scaffolds for enhanced ascorbic acid and ascorbate oxidase sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bState Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the synthesis of QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe and its application for sensing.

Herein, a ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe was successfully fabricated through incapsulating quantum dots (QDs) and carbon dots (CDs) into the cavities of zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 by self-assembly, which generated QDs/CDs@MOFs. The QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe combined the properties of metal − organic frameworks (MOFs) and ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe, thus it possessed following advantages including strong adsorption and enrichment ability for the analytes, and the ability of eliminating interferences of environmental and instrumental factors. The fluorescence of QDs loaded in the cages of ZIF-8 was initially quenched by mercury ions. However, mercury ions could be reduced to mercury atom by ascorbic acid (AA), leading to the restoration of QDs fluorescence. Moreover, under the catalysis of ascorbate oxidase (AAO), AA was oxidized to dehydroascorbic acid that couldn’t reduce mercury ions, resulting in the fluorescence of QDs quenched again. Simultaneously, the fluorescence of CDs loaded in the ZIF-8 was not affected under above procedures. Hence, with QDs serving as reporter signal and CDs acting as stable reference signal, a ratio fluorescence platform was constructed for sensitive determination of AA and AAO with the linear calibration in the range of 0.01−0.2 μM and 0.05–4 U·L−1 and the detection limit of 4 nM and 0.02 U·L−1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal − organic frameworks (MOFs) are well-ordered porous hybrid materials synthesized by the assembly between metal ions and organic linkers in appropriate solvents [1]. During past decades, MOFs have been elaborately designed and prepared, and the tunable superiorities of MOFs promote them excellent candidates in various ﬁelds, for instance gas adsorption and storage [2], energy storage [3], heterogeneous catalysis [4], drug delivery [5], and optoelectronics [6]. Recently, MOFs have been successfully investigated for the fabrication of nanoprobes on the basis of loading ﬂuorescent species in MOFs [7–9]. The ultrahigh porosities and tunable pores make MOFs promising host matrices for various ﬂuorescent species. Moreover, it was demonstrated that MOFs based nanoprobes not only could prevent ﬂuorescent species from aggregation to improve the properties of the species, but also could enrich the analytes from the environment to amplify analysis signals [10]. Therefore, encapsulating fluorescent species into the cavities of MOFs is an attractive strategy for fabricating MOF-based nanoprobes with multiple excellent properties.

Zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs) such as ZIF-8, as one of the most interesting representative MOFs, feature several remarkable advantages such as ultrahigh porosity, aqueous dispensability, large surface area, easy preparation, and excellent ability for host matrix [11]. Up to now, a variety of ﬂuorescent dyes or nanoparticles, including rhodamine [12], methylene blue [7], carbon dots [13], gold nanoclusters [14], copper nanoclusters [15], and silver nanoclusters [16], have been successfully incapsulated into ZIFs cavities to develop MOF-based nanoprobes. These nanoprobes were utilized to detect metal ions, DNA, and small molecules with satisfactory results [17–20]. However, most of the MOF-based nanoprobes suffered from interferences caused by environment and instruments owing to its single wavelength dependent signal. On the contrary, the studies on ratiometric nanoprobes based on MOFs remain in an infantile primary stage, which are urgently need to further development.

Quantum dots (QDs) have gained tremendous attention in the fields of biosensing and bioimaging owing to its merits such as high extinction coeﬃcients, tunable emission spectra, broad absorption spectra, high quantum yield, and high photostability [21–23]. Recently, carbon dots (CDs), as a novel class quantum sized fluorescent nanoparticles, have ignited enormous research interest due to its unique properties including low toxicity, good water solubility, easy and green synthesis, and high photoluminescence stability [24,25]. Hence, the integration of the fascinating properties of QDs and CDs for the purpose of fabricating nanoprobe is quite promising. Nevertheless, both QDs and CDs easy to agglomerate or precipitate during analytes detection, which limit their further applications [26,27]. Alternatively, the stability of QDs and CDs can be improved when they combine with porous MOFs, which not only prevent their agglomeration or precipitation but also improve their properties [28,29]. Besides, the porous MOFs provide plenty of binding sites or cavities for anchoring QDs and CDs. Therefore, it is fascinating to encapsulate QDs and CDs into MOFs to construct ratiometric nanoprobe with the charming properties of QDs, CDs, and MOFs.

Ascorbic acid (AA) is one of the most significant nutrients in human body owing to its functions such as free radical scavenging, antioxidant effects, immunity improvement and cell development [30]. Ascorbic acid oxidase (AAO), as a copper containing enzyme, can catalyze the redox reaction between oxygen and AA to produce dehydroascorbic acid (DHA) and water [31]. AAO widely distributed in plants, mainly existed in the cytoplasm as a free enzyme and located in the cell wall in the forms of membrane binding, which can regulate redox state and growth of plant cells [32]. To date, there are several methods for AA analysis including chromatography [33], electrochemistry [34], colorimetry [35], chemiluminescence [36], and fluorescence methods [37]. Nevertheless, the methods related to AAO activity detection were rarely reported. Han et al. reported the AA and AAO activity analysis using manganese (II)-doped zinc/germanium oxide nanoparticles [38]. Liu et al. realized the detection of AA and AAO activity through controlling the growth and aggregation of DNA-templated gold/silver nanoclusters [39]. Wang et al. combined the properties of fluorescent carbon dots and o-phenylenediamine to realize ratio-dependent fluorescence and colorimetric determination of AA and AAO activity [40]. Although, it is still urgent to develop a simple, sensitive and selective platform for AAO activity analysis.

Herein, as shown in Scheme 1 , a facile one-pot strategy was designed to prepare ratiometric fluorescence QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe by the coordination-directed self-assembly of ZIF-8 in the presence of CDs and QDs. The QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe had strong absorption ability which could selectively capture and accumulate target analytes owing to MOFs excellent properties. In the meantime, the dual emission property of the QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe endowed it with the superiorities of ratiometric sensors, which can eliminate the interferences resulted from the instability of environment and instruments. Moreover, the utilization of MOFs to capsulate QDs and CDs inside of their cavities could prevent the agglomeration or precipitation of QDs and CDs. As displayed in Scheme 1, red emissive QDs of the nanoprobe was quenched in the presence of Hg2+ through electron transfer and ion-binding process. However, the addition of AA could recover the QDs fluorescence of nanoprobe due to the redox reaction between Hg2+ and AA. Because AA could be oxidized under the catalysis of AAO, resulting in the reduction reaction of Hg2+ intensively suppressed, QDs fluorescence of the nanoprobe was quenched again. The CDs fluorescence in the nanoprobe nearly maintained constant throughout the process, acting as a stable reference fluorescent signal. Hence, the sensitive and selective detection of AA and AAO activity was realized through the ratiometric MOF-based nanoprobe. Meanwhile, owing to the MOFs remarkable properties, our MOFs-based nanoprobe could enrich Hg2+ and thus improved the nanoprobe sensitivity for Hg2+, which further largely elevated the sensitivity for AA and AAO activity due to their detection based on Hg2+ quenching.

Ascorbic acid (AA),citric acid (CA), tartaric acid (TA), uric acid (UA), dopamine (DA), urea, sialic acid (SA),sodium borohydride, glucose, galactose, adenosine triphosphate (ATP), and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) were procured from Aladdin Reagent Company (Shanghai, China).Alkaline phosphatase (ALP), adenosine deaminase (ADA), tyrosinase (TYR), alpha-glucosidase (α-Glu), exonuclease III (Exo III),creatinine (Cre), pepsin (Pep), and Choline (Cho) were purchased from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China).Alanine (Ala), Valine (Val), Glutamate (Glu), phenylalanine (Phe), methionine (Met), histidine (His), arginine (Arg),glutamine (Gln), leucine (Leu), and proline (Pro) were obtained from Beijing Dingguo Biotechnology Company (Beijing, China). Fructose, maltose, mercapto acetic acid (TGA) and mercuric chloride (HgCl2) were purchased from macklin Chemical Co. All of the regents and chemicals in the present work were acquired and utilized without any alteration.

The thioglycolic acid (TGA) capped CdTe QDs was synthesized according to previous reported literature [41]. Typically, 40 mg tellurium power and 80 mg NaBH4 was mixed in 5 mL distilled water under nitrogen protected. After the mixture reacted for 20 min at 60 °C under nitrogen atmosphere and the mixture color changed from black to transparent, the NaHTe solution was prepared. The fresh prepared oxygen-free NaHTe solution (5 mL) was rapidly transferred into nitrogen saturated pH = 12.0 CdCl2 (1.5 mmol) solution in the presence of TGA (3 mmol). The TGA-capped CdTe QDs with red emission was prepared after above mixture reacted at 90 °C for 27 h. The as prepared CdTe QDs was concentrated through precipitated by ethanol and centrifugated at 9000 rpm for 10 min. After the precipitation washed by ethanol three times, distilled water was added to dissolve CdTe QDs and the solution was kept in refrigerator for further utilization. The fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of QDs was 10.59 %, employing rhodamine 6 G as references.

The CDs was prepared based on previous method [42]. In brief, 1.5 g citric acid and 3 g urea was dispersed in 15 mL distilled water. Then, the above solution was transferred into a Teﬂon reactor and treated at 160 °C for 4 h. After cooling down, the obtained CDs solution was precipitated by adding ethanol and centrifugation at 10,000 rpm about 15 min was performed to collect the precipitated CDs. The precipitation was washed three times to get rid of residual chemicals and then CDs was obtained after it dried at 50 °C for 1 day. The obtained CDs was dispersed into 15 mL deionized water and then stored at 4 °C refrigerator for further application. The fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of CDs was 21.92 %, employing quinine sulfate as reference.

The QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe was synthesized through a facile one-pot approach based on previous literature with minor modification [43]. Specifically, a solution of 2-methylimidazole (2-MIM, 4 mmol) in 7.5 mL methanol was mixed with 2 mL QDs (1 mg/mL) and 30 u L CDs (10 mg/mL). Then, a solution of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (0.5 mmol) was transferred into above solution and the mixture was reacted for 4 h. The QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe was obtained after the suspension was separated by centrifugation and washed by methanol for several times. Finally, the QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe was dried at 50 °C in oven for later use. Pure ZIF-8 was synthesized by similar method except adding QDs and CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of AA and AAO activity
ce:section.ce:para: For the detection of AA, 20 mg QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe was dissolved in 20 mL distilled water and sonicated about 3 min to get a homogeneous stock solution. Then 20 μL various concentrations of AA and 20 μL Hg2+ (4 μM) was incubated at room temperature for 5 min. Thereafter, 20 u L QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe and 340 μL pH = 7.8 Tris-HCl were added and the mixture was vortexed for 60 s. Finally, the ﬂuorescence emission spectra was acquired with the excitation wavelength of 340 nm.

For the detection of AAO, 8 u L different amount of AAO, 4 μL pH = 7.8 Tris-HCl, and 8 u L AA (100 μM) were mixed together and incubated at 37 °C for 40 min. After 20 μL Hg2+ (4 μM) was added into above mixture and incubated for 5 min, 20 μL QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe and 340 μL pH = 7.8 Tris-HCl were added and the mixture was vortexed for 60 s. Finally, the ﬂuorescence emission spectra was acquired with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of AAO activity in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: The human blood samples were offered from the Changchun China-Japan Union Hospital. The human blood samples were firstly pretreated by filtration using 0.45 μm membrane, and then serum samples were obtained after it centrifugated at 10,000 rpm for 20 min. Then different levels of AAO were mixed into the serum samples, which were subsequently diluted 20-fold for later experiments. The determination process for AAO activity in the serum samples was identical with the process described above.

The QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe was synthesized through capturing QDs and CDs into the cavities of ZIF-8 during its self-assembly through a facile one-pot method, which was demonstrated in Scheme 1. Figure S1 displayed the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence excitation, emission spectra of CDs and QDs. It can be seen that the excitation spectra of QDs was successive and the maximum excitation wavelengths of CDs is 370 nm. Considering that both CDs and QDs emissive intensities were relatively high when excited at 340 nm, we selected 340 nm as excitation wavelength during the detection procedures. The maximum emission peaks of CDs and QDs were located at 455 and 615 nm. Figure S2 showed that the fluorescence intensities of QDs and CDs loaded in the ZIF-8 were enhanced with the increase of their content. However, the fluorescence intensity of QDs encapsulated in ZIf-8 increased slowly when its concentration was more than 0.1 mg/mL. For the purpose of the fluorescence intensity of QDs slightly higher than CDs in the ZIF-8, 0.1 mg/mL of QDs and 0.015 mg/mL of CDs were selected for the synthesis of QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the as-prepared QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe manifested good dispersibility in aqueous solution, and its solution color changed from white to light yellow under daylight after encapsulation. The luminescent color of the nanoprobe appeared red when illuminated by ultraviolet lamp. As shown in Fig. 1B, there was no fluorescence emission peak appeared for ZIF-8, which was consistent with previous reports [44,45]. In comparison with the ﬂuorescent emission spectra of QDs, CDs, and QDs/CDs, the ﬂuorescence spectra of nanoprobe (Fig. 1B) was nearly identical with that of CDs and QDs, which illustrated that the ZIF-8 served as scaffolds to encapsulate QDs and CDs and had no adverse effect on the fluorescence properties of QDs and CDs. Therefore, the fluorescence properties of CDs and QDs were well retained after encapsulation in ZIF-8 and the fluorescent emission of QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe originated from CDs and QDs instead of ZIF-8.

To prove that QDs and CDs were incapsulated into the cavities of ZIF-8, instead of absorbed on the surface of ZIF-8, the QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe was thoroughly washed by distilled water. After centrifugation, the supernatant fluorescence intensity was almost zero, whereas the QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe fluorescence intensity kept nearly same as compared with that before washing (Figure S3). Hence, the QDs and CDs were fully encapsulated into the cages of ZIF-8 and the QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe was successfully synthesized. The fluorescence stability (Figure S4) of QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe was investigated by measuring its fluorescence spectra every five days, and the fluorescence intensity was identical within 20 days, indicating its excellent stability.

To further demonstrate the successful prepared of QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe, TEM characterizations were performed for ZIF-8 and the nanoprobe. As exhibited in Figure S5 and Fig. 1C, both ZIF-8 and QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe exhibited regular rhombic dodecahedral structure with an average diameter of 45 nm. HR-TEM image (Fig. 1D) of QDs/CDs@MOFs suggested that QDs and CDs were successfully incapsulated in ZIF-8. Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) for pure ZIF-8 and QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe were conducted to verify that QDs and CDs encapsulation didn’t influence the structure of ZIF-8. As displayed in Fig. 2 A, the reflection located in 2θ of 7.3° was ascribed to the (110) plane, which was in accordance with the rhombic dodecahedron of the nanocrystals of ZIF-8 [46], and the similar diffraction peaks in PXRD of pure ZIF-8 and QDs/CDs@MOFs were observed, which might due to the fact that there was no interaction among QDs, CDs, and ZIF-8. The results indicated that the crystalline integrity of ZIF-8 was not affected after encapsulating QDs and CDs.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was also performed for characterization of QDs, CDs, ZIF-8, and QDs/CDs@MOFs. As exhibited in Fig. 2B, the spectrum of QDs/CDs@MOFs was nearly same as that of ZIF-8, and the characteristic peaks of QDs and CDs were not appeared in the spectrum of QDs/CDs@MOFs. This phenomenon might be owing to the fact that QDs and CDs were incorporated into ZIF-8 cavities and the amount of QDs and CDs in the nanoprobe were quite small, which didn’t affect the characteristic peaks of ZIF-8.

To confirm that the MOF structure of the nanoprobe had the capability to enrich the analyte and enhance the detection sensitivity, 0.2 μM of Hg2+ was mixed into QDs/CDs and QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe solution, respectively. Fig. 3 A displayed that the ﬂuorescence quenching intensity (ΔF) at 615 nm of QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe was much stronger than that of QDs/CDs, which might be attributed to the porous structure of ZIF-8 and numerous imidazole ligands on ZIF-8 absorbing Hg2+ [47]. As a result, the MOF-based nanoprobe can accumulate analyte and improve the sensitivity for Hg2+, and thus the sensor sensitivity for AA and AAO can be also improved owing to their detection based on Hg2+ quenching.

The feasibility for sensing AA and AAO were systematically studied. As seen in Fig. 3B, the ﬂuorescence intensity at 615 nm of the nanoprobe was effectively quenched by Hg2+ and the fluorescence peak at 455 nm almost kept invariable. Nevertheless, when Hg2+ and AA coexisted, the fluorescence at 615 nm was efficiently recovered. Furthermore, after AA was incubated with AAO and mixed with Hg2+, the fluorescence of the nanoprobe at 615 nm was quenched again. These results indicated that the MOF-based nanoprobe could be utilized for the detection of AA and AAO based on “off-on” and “off-on-off” strategy and the QDs/CDs@MOFs ratiometric nanoprobe offered a build-in correction which could eliminate interferences of environmental and instrumental factors and increase detection accuracy.

To study the sensing mechanism of the nanoprobe, a series of experiments were systematically investigated. The mechanism of the quenching effect of Hg2+ on QDs/CDs@MOFs was basically similar with that of the quenching of QDs by Hg2+. As exhibited in Fig. 3C, after addition of Hg2+, the absorption peak of QDs at 595 nm was red shifted about 20 nm, which suggested the size of QDs increased due to the size-quantized HgS generated on QDs surface that led to QDs fluorescence quenching [48]. However, the absorption peak was blue shifted to original after AA addition because of Hg2+ reduced by AA. The fluorescence lifetime of QDs/CDs@MOFs at 615 nm was also investigated owing to the fluorescence intensity at 455 nm kept stable. As seen in Fig. 3D, the lifetime of QDs/CDs@MOFs was reduced from 24.97 ns to 10.68 ns after Hg2+ addition, which was attributed to the electron transfer from QDs of the nanoprobe to Hg2+ [49]. However, the lifetime time was restored from 10.68 ns to 20.99 ns after AA addition, which implied the redox reation between AA and Hg2+ existed. As a consequence, both ion-binding (HgS) and electron transfer led to QDs/CDs@MOFs fluorescence quenching. The redox reaction between Hg2+ and AA was further confirmed by UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra. As seen in Figure S6A, the absorption peak at 265 nm of AA was blue shifted about 10 nm and extensively decreased after mixing with Hg2+, which suggested a redox reaction between AA and Hg2+ occurred. Besides, Figure S6B exhibited that there was no fluorescence peak for the solution of OPD (o-phenylenediamine), AA + OPD, and Hg2+ + OPD, but a strong fluorescence peak at 435 nm appeared for the solution of Hg2+ + AA + OPD, which demonstrated that AA could be oxidized to dehydroascorbic acid (DHA) in the redox reaction with Hg2+ and then DHA was reacted with OPD to produce fluorescent quinoxaline derivative (QXD) [50]. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe at 615 nm could be restored because the redox reaction between AA and Hg2+ led to the lack of Hg2+. Nevertheless, AA was oxidized after incubated with AAO, which resulted in the redox reaction between AA and Hg2+ blocked, and thus the fluorescence intensity at 615 nm was quenched by Hg2+ again. Therefore, the MOF-based nanoprobe could be applied to the determination of AA and AAO.

To obtain satisfactory detection performance for AA, a series of parameters were optimized such as Hg2+ concentration, incubation time between Hg2+ and the nanoprobe, pH and reaction time between Hg2+ and AA. As shown in Figure S7, the fluorescence intensity of nanoprobe at 615 nm decreased with the increasement of Hg2+ concentrations and the value of F615/F455 kept stable above 0.2 μM Hg2+ which was selected for AA detection. Figure S8B showed that pH of 7.8 were suitable for the detection of AA. Figure S8A, C showed that the nanoprobe fluorescence could be quenched by Hg2+ immediately, and the reaction between Hg2+ and AA was finished within 1 min. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the ratiometric nanoprobe was applied for AA determination. As exhibited in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of QDs/CDs@MOFs/Hg2+ system at 615 nm was gradually enhanced with the concentrations of AA increasing from 0 to 0.3 μM, while the fluorescence intensity at 455 nm kept almost identical. Therefore, an excellent linear calibration (R2 = 0.996) for the ﬂuorescent intensity ratio (F615/F455) and AA concentrations was acquired from Fig. 4B. The regression linear equation was F615/F455 = 0.532 + 4.580[AA] in the ranges of 0.01−0.2 μM with the detection limit (LOD) of 4 nM calculated by 3σ/slope (σ represents the standard deviation of blank signals (n = 11)). As displayed in Table S1, a satisfactory linear range was obtained and the LOD of the strategy was lower than the majority of previously reported methods, indicating the ultrahigh sensitivity of the strategy for AA analysis. Figure S9 exhibited that the ﬂuorescent intensity ratio (F615/F455) increased obviously with the addition of 0.2 μM AA, whereas the value of (F615/F455) displayed no obvious changes with other substances (including some reductants) at even much higher concentrations, illustrating the high selectivity of the sensing system for AA analysis.

In order to improve the sensitivity for AAO activity analysis, some vital factors including AA concentration, the enzymatic reaction time, pH, and temperature were optimized. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F615/F455) reached plateau at 0.2 μM AA (Fig. 4B), so 0.2 μM AA were selected for AAO activity analysis. From Figure S10, enzymatic reaction time of 40 min, reaction pH value of 7.8, and reaction temperature of 37 °C were chosen for the determination of AAO activity. Under the optimized assay conditions, the fluorescent emission spectra of QDs/CDs@MOFs/Hg2+/AA system treated with various amounts of AAO were recorded. As depicted in Fig. 4C, the nanoprobe fluorescence at 615 nm progressively decreased, while the fluorescence at 455 nm maintained constant with the amount of AAO increasing from 0 to 6 U·L−1. The calibration curve was calculated by utilizing the ﬂuorescent intensity ratio (F615/F455) and AAO levels to evaluate the capability of the sensing system for the quantitative detection of AAO activity (Fig. 4D). The regression linear equation was F615/F455 = 1.302 – 0.189[AAO] from 0.05 to 4 U·L−1 (R2 = 0.990), and the LOD was 0.02 U·L−1 calculated according to 3σ/k (σ is the standard deviation of blank signals (n = 11) and k is the slope of F615/F455 versus AA concentration). The stability of the sensing system was also studied. As exhibited in Figure S11, the value of F615/F455 kept stable within 24 h for QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe in solution and in the present of Hg2+, Hg2+ + AA or Hg2+ + AA + AAO, indicating the good stability of the QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe for detection of AA and AAO. From Table S2, it could be observed that the strategy exhibited a comparable linear range and a much lower LOD than most of previous reports, demonstrating the feasibility of the proposed ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe for AAO activity analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, anti-interference and real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity and anti-interference capability are considered as vital characteristics in assessment of practicality of the sensing platform, particularly for the development of sensing strategy in complicated biological samples analysis. Hence, for evaluating the selectivity and anti-interference capability of the strategy for AAO analysis, the effects of the potential interferential substances (such as bioenzymes, biological molecules, and common ions) on the selectivity and anti-interference of the strategy were systematically studied. As displayed in Fig. 5 A, the value of the ﬂuorescent intensity ratio (F615/F455) sharply decreased at low levels of AAO (4 U·L−1), whereas the value of (F615/F455) displayed no obvious changes with other substances at even much higher concentrations. It could be observed from the interference assay (Fig. 5B) that the system response to AAO activity was identical with coexisting species present. These observations well illustrated that the sensing platform had excellent selectivity and remarkable anti-interference for the detection of AAO activity in real samples.

To further evaluate the practical application of the sensing strategy, the MOF-based nanoprobe was applied to AAO activity analysis in human serum with standard addition method. As displayed in Table 1 , the average recoveries of AAO activity were in the range of 96–106 % with the RSD of 2.26–4.56 %, indicating the practicability and reliability of the sensing platform for AAO activity analysis in human serum.

In summary, a ratio fluorescent nanoprobe was strategically fabricated through a facile one-pot strategy by incapsulating QDs and CDs in the cavities of ZIF-8 during its self-assembly. The MOF-based nanoprobe combined the strong adsorption capability of MOFs with superior properties of the incorporated fluorescent materials. Thus, the as-prepared QDs/CDs@MOFs nanoprobe had the advantages such as inhibiting encapsulated ﬂuorescent materials aggregation, accumulating the analytes, enhancing the detection sensitivity, and eliminating environmental and instrumental interferences. The red fluorescence QDs of the nanoprobe could be quenched by Hg2+ through ion-binding and electron transfer. On the basis of the redox reaction between Hg2+ and AA and enzymic catalytic reaction of AAO, a ratio fluorescent platform was successfully constructed for AA and AAO analysis based on “off-on” and “off-on-off” strategy, respectively. The sensing system exhibited good selectivity and anti-interference, which performed well in real samples analysis. It can be envisioned that the sensing system has the potential to provide an innovative pathway for constructing nanoprobe and sensing various target analytes.

Junyang Chen: Conceptualization, Data curation, Validation, Investigation, Software, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Shan Jiang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Mengke Wang: Formal analysis. Xiaolei Xie: Software. Xingguang Su: Methodology, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21775052 and 21575048) and the Science and Technology Development project of Jilin province, China (No.20180414013GH).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.129910.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Nature

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Junyang Chen is pursuing his PhD study under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). His research is mainly focused on the synthesis of quantum dots and mental nanoclusters and their application on sensing.

Shan Jiang is currently carrying out her Doctor's degree under the guidance of Professor Wenjing Tian in Jilin University (China). Her research is focused on the synthesis and application of covalent organic framework for sensing.

Mengke Wang is currently carrying out her Doctor's degree under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). Her research is mainly focused on the mental nanoclusters and quantum dots and their applications for biosensors.

Xiaolei Xie is currently carrying out his MS degree under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). His research is mainly focused on the synthesis of single-atom nanozyme and their application on sensing.

Xingguang Su is a professor at the Department of Analytical Chemistry at the College of Chemistry, Jilin University. She received her MS degree from Jilin University (China) in 1992 and her PhD degree from Jilin University (China) in 1999. Her research focuses on the synthesis, characterization, functionalization and application of quantum dots and quantum dots-tagged microspheres in biomedicine.
